notes_for_reviewer: This moment captures the duality of celebrating a milestone ('you graduated') while still feeling the ongoing nature of survivorship.

moment_text: I think it's important to advocate. There's so many different layers for me because of where I come from, where I'm from. I'm from an urban area, and the buses that ride through here are white women. Um, and I live in a black Puerto Rican area that doesn't represent the young girls that are at the bus stops. That doesn't represent the young girls at the community college. Um, and I think it's important for. People who look like me to see that representation that she went through, that she's going through, that she's surviving. Okay, I won't be scared to go get this mammogram. I won't be scared to. And it's just, it's better conversations to have, um, if I'm having it with my people in my community because I know how to talk to them and they understand the jargon, and I can just tell 'em, go, go get your breast checked. And also for my daughter, number one, I think. For her. It's just always, I just always want her to be comfortable speaking about her breast and speaking about her health or just, just speaking up in general. I think as women, young women, we just have to speak up and don't take no for an answer. And I think more so it was probably how I was raised. Um, don't take no for an answer and don't let nobody walk over you just advocating. It just makes it easier for the people behind you to walk for that crawl that you had to go through. So that's why it's important.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains very strong agency signals (self_advocacy, speaking_up) and barrier signals (education_gap, stigma) within a community system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: This is a powerful moment about the intersection of representation, community health, and intergenerational empowerment.

moment_text: I want, um, what I want everyone to know is, is that this is your journey. Whatever path you choose is your path. If you decide to do this or that, that's your decision. That don't listen to no one, because at the end of the day, you have to live with that decision. Um, whether it's going through chemo or going through surgery or whatever. Life, your body. You do that, you make those decisions and don't let a doctor or anyone change that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong agency signals (self_advocacy, making_decisions) and emotional signals (empowerment) in the context of a healthcare provider interaction.

moment_text: I would like to say life. Oh, survivorship, life after cancer. It is not a walk in the park, but it is a life. It is something to be grateful for. Um, and if I would tell you two years ago, I was in the darkest place ever. I did not see a light at the end of the tunnel. I did not think that I would be here. I, and honestly, I didn't even think that I would be sharing my story or any of that. I thought it was the weirdest thing to do. And I think that life after Cancer, it, it can be a bliss, but it, it's, it's a great, it's a, it can be a great life.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong emotional signals (hopelessness, hope) and agency signals (sharing_story) describing the lived experience of survivorship.
